name: Book of Law
description: The Book of the Law (Liber AL vel Legis) is the central sacred text of Thelema, a religion and philosophy founded in the early 20th century by Aleister Crowley. Proponents claim it was dictated to Crowley in Cairo, Egypt, in 1904 by a discarnate entity named Aiwass. The book outlines principles of Thelemic law: "Do what thou wilt shall be the whole of the Law" and "Love is the law, love under will." It posits a new Aeon, the Aeon of Horus, replacing the Aeon of Osiris and heralding a time of individual self-realization and freedom. The book is divided into three chapters, each supposedly dictated on a different day. Crowley himself interpreted the text throughout his life, and his commentaries form an integral part of Thelemic doctrine. Mainstream scholarship generally views the Book of the Law as a product of Crowley's own subconscious, influenced by his occult studies, philosophical readings, and personal experiences. Psychological analyses often point to Crowley's ego and desire for religious innovation as driving forces behind the book's creation. Historians also note the context of the late 19th and early 20th century occult revival, which provided a fertile ground for such claims. Skeptics highlight the lack of verifiable evidence for the existence of Aiwass and question the book's internal consistency and coherence. The Thelemic interpretation emphasizes symbolic and esoteric meanings, while critics view the text as often obscure, contradictory, and reliant on subjective interpretation.
subtitle: Central text of Thelema, dictated by Aiwass.

claims.claim: The Book of the Law was dictated by a discarnate entity named Aiwass.
claims.evidence: Crowley's testimony and his detailed account of the dictation process in his diaries and autobiographical writings.
claims.criticism: No independent verification exists for the existence of Aiwass or the dictation process. Mainstream science rejects the possibility of communication with discarnate entities.

claims.claim: The book heralds a new Aeon of Horus, characterized by individual freedom and self-realization.
claims.evidence: Interpretation of the book's text and Crowley's commentaries, which emphasize the importance of individual will and the rejection of traditional morality.
claims.criticism: The concept of Aeons is not scientifically valid and is based on subjective interpretation of symbolic and mythological systems. The potential for abuse of the 'Do what thou wilt' principle is a frequent concern.

claims.claim: The book contains hidden codes and numerical patterns that reveal deeper meanings.
claims.evidence: Thelemic scholars have identified various numerical and symbolic patterns in the text, such as gematria and Qabbalistic correspondences.
claims.criticism: These patterns are often based on selective interpretation and can be found in almost any text if one looks hard enough. Critics argue that these interpretations are subjective and lack objective validity.

keyFigures.name: Richard Kaczynski
keyFigures.role: Biographer of Aleister Crowley and scholar of Thelema

timeline.event: Alleged dictation of The Book of the Law in Cairo, Egypt
Crowley publishes the first edition of The Book of the Law
Aleister Crowley dies, leaving behind a complex legacy and a growing Thelemic movement

sources.author: Richard Kaczynski

sources.title: Richard Kaczynski
